cards.definition: a quantity with magnitude (size) only — e.g. speed, distance, mass, temperature
a quantity with both magnitude and direction — e.g. velocity, displacement, force, acceleration
the distance travelled per unit of time — a scalar quantity (Speed = Distance ÷ Time)
the speed of an object in a given direction — a vector quantity
the rate of change of velocity — a vector quantity (Acceleration = Change in velocity ÷ Time)
an object remains at rest or moves at constant velocity unless acted on by a resultant force
the resultant force on an object equals its mass multiplied by its acceleration (F = ma)
every action has an equal and opposite reaction — forces always act in pairs on different objects
the total distance a vehicle travels before stopping (Stopping Distance = Thinking Distance + Braking Distance)
the product of an object's mass and velocity (p = mv) — a vector quantity; total momentum is conserved in a closed system
ways energy is held by objects — kinetic, gravitational potential, elastic potential, thermal, chemical, nuclear, electromagnetic
the energy an object has due to its motion (KE = ½mv²) — depends on mass and speed
the energy stored in an object due to its height above the ground (GPE = mgh)
energy cannot be created or destroyed — it can only be transferred from one store to another
the proportion of input energy transferred to a useful output (Efficiency = Useful output energy ÷ Total input energy × 100%)
energy being transferred to the surroundings in a less useful form — e.g. heat and sound from a car engine
the rate at which energy is transferred (Power = Energy transferred ÷ Time, measured in watts)
the energy required to raise 1 kg of a substance by 1°C — water has a high specific heat capacity
energy from sources that will not run out — e.g. solar, wind, tidal, geothermal
energy from sources that will eventually run out — e.g. coal, oil, natural gas, nuclear fuel
a wave where the oscillation is perpendicular (at right angles) to the direction of energy transfer — e.g. light, water waves
a wave where the oscillation is parallel to the direction of energy transfer — e.g. sound waves
the distance from one wave crest to the next — measured in metres
the number of complete waves passing a point per second — measured in hertz (Hz)
the maximum displacement of a wave from its rest position — related to the energy of the wave
the speed at which a wave travels (Wave speed = Frequency × Wavelength)
the family of transverse waves travelling at the same speed in a vacuum — from radio waves (longest wavelength) to gamma rays (shortest)
the change in direction of a wave as it passes from one medium to another due to a change in speed
when a wave hits a boundary at an angle greater than the critical angle and is reflected back — the basis of fibre optics
radiation with enough energy to remove electrons from atoms — gamma rays, X-rays, and UV are ionising. Can damage DNA and cause cancer
a particle made of 2 protons and 2 neutrons — strongly ionising, stopped by paper or a few cm of air
a fast-moving electron emitted from the nucleus — moderately ionising, stopped by a few mm of aluminium
electromagnetic radiation from the nucleus — weakly ionising, only stopped by thick lead or concrete
the time taken for the count rate of a radioactive source to halve — or for half the atoms to decay
splitting a heavy nucleus (e.g. uranium) into two smaller nuclei — releases a large amount of energy and two or three neutrons
joining two light nuclei together to form a heavier nucleus — releases more energy than fission; the energy source of stars

cards.keyword: the life cycle of a star
cards.definition: stars form from nebulae → main sequence → red giant/supergiant → white dwarf/neutron star/black hole (depending on mass)
the scientific theory that the universe began from an extremely hot, dense point about 13.8 billion years ago and has been expanding since
the increase in wavelength of light from distant galaxies — shows the universe is expanding (galaxies are moving away from us)
low-level radiation found throughout the universe — key evidence for the Big Bang theory
